speaker: interviewer
text: So in the ghetto, why was it that your family was not taken to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the German used to come to my father. You should make shoes for the horses. And then they will take us to the ghetto, my father would lose the little shop to make shoes for the horses. They didn't took us-- send us away. They didn't took away. And my father used to do it for the German, make shoes for the horses and to put the shoes on the horses. They we-- they used to come, the Germans themselves, used to come and use the little shop to, for example, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to fix for themself blacksmith work. That's why they left us without not taking to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your brothers and sisters and the rest of your family doing during those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first-- first sister, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] used to make the-- what do they call this-- for the shoes? I don't know what do you call this in English? How do--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you say it in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH] the [POLISH]-- [POLISH] the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The soles of the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soles of the shoes, yeah. He used to work. They were very busy because the Polish people knew they're going to kill the Jews and they came with the sheets of leather, she should make the-- these things--

speaker: interviewer
text: Soles.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the shoes. Because, when they killed them, they wouldn't have such people to do it because Polish people they are-- no-- no-- not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- they don't-- they don't know how to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was going on in 1940, 1940 to September 1942, and they were very busy. And they used to bring him food, flour. They use to bring all kind to eat. We didn't suffer from hunger. We didn't suffer from hunger. Later, they-- he had all the machines to make the-- these-- these things--

speaker: interviewer
text: The soles?

speaker: interviewee
text: The soles. Yes. It was nice. We didn't know they're gonna kill us. We didn't know. For example, they took us out-- took out the women and the children. And I said I'm gonna go with my mother because I'm such a mechanic. You know. I'm gonna go to work and I'm going to support them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was September, 1942. We didn't know they gonna took us to kill. They took my father and three brothers, went to the ghetto, stay in the ghetto. I, with my mother and the sisters went together, because I told, they're gonna-- I'm gonna go to work and I'm going to support them, which was a good idea. When we came to Miedzyrzec-- it was the town. The houses were empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us in a wagon. Horses-- you know, horses and a wagon. They took us to Miedzyrecz. And they took us there. And when we came to the city, they let us go into the houses. The houses were empty. There was a lot there was a lot of feathers from the-- from the blankets the Jewish people used to have there flying all around, because they ripped them and they fly-- they used to fly around. And we were there five days. Five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we were there five days to cry and the bastards-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's all right, I say? The bastards, they came and they chased us out from these-- from the houses and the big place they called-- a [POLISH] a [POLISH], they called it. The Jewish [POLISH]-- that's-- how to call in English? How to call in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Market.

speaker: interviewee
text: Market.

speaker: interviewer
text: Market.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they let-- tell us, sit down on the floor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] German was standing by-- a lot of Jewish people try-- there was a church in market. And a lot of Jewish people run away to the church. And the German killed them right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that with your own eyes?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my own eyes. They killed them. Later came out the priest-- some of them run away, the-- the Germans didn't saw it. When we were there all day till at night, they took us to the train. I didn't want to go in the train right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said it's gonna get dark. Maybe I'm going to run away, not to go into the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were left about 50 people. Couldn't go in no more because the train was full. I understand very well German. They said, we're gonna kill them. The rest which didn't go in, we're gonna kill them. I understand German. I pushed her in myself inside, in the train. The rest, they killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had to go into the window. I was by the door. I had to go into the window. It was impossible to go into the window. You have to go on the top of the heads on the people, because there were so many people you can't-- you don't-- you don't have space to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the rest of your family in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family wasn't-- wait, I forgot something-- OK-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My mother, the sisters, and the kids, they took them away five days before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They separated the young boys, the young girls. They put them in synagogue. It was a big synagogue, beautiful synagogue. They put us in the synagogue. And they captured us five days without food. Without water and without food. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We should lose our strength and fifth day, they took us out. The Jewish police came, and they brought over bread. Loaf of bread. I captured a bread. And a German was standing by. He ran over the German and killed one Jew. The bullets went into young boy in the hand, and the bullets went to me. It right-- hit me. It hit me very-- very bad, very sharp. But it not hit me, just hit me. And they took us to the train. They took us to the train. I didn't want to go to the train, run away. I was full of life because I had a bread. I ate bread. And became dark. 5 o'clock, it was September, end of September, about at 6 o'clock got dark. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And like the German came over and said kill them. They should kill us. The people would. I am so sick. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They should kill us on the spot. OK. I understand what they said. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- I pushed me in the train. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was by the door. The door was locked-- closed. I said, please, people, let me go to the window. Maybe I will help some people escape, too. I went to the window and I-- it was a rail in the middle of the window. I broke the rail, one side, just one side. And I jumped onto the train. I jumped onto the train. I was watching the train. The train was going about 200 speed limit. The train was going. I was watching. They should be a-- a hole, and I jumped out into hole. I lost my mind. My mind-- something hurt-- hurt me and got help came a rain. When the rain started to pouring, it was leaking. And we became wet. And I came to myself. I remember a Jewish boy jumped close where I-- where I jumped.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a soldier, a Polish soldier with a Polish [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, I saw to him. I was scared. I was afraid. Are you a Jew? I told him. I said, yes, I'm Jewish. I jumped from the train, too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, he said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we can't stay here, by the rail, because the German, when it gets daytime, the German comes and the rest-- there a lot of people jumped-- they cut the hands, they cut the feet, and the Germans killed them. Shoot-- shoot them. I didn't, was scared nothing. I went to a garden with cabbage. And I laid down with the cabbage, I digged a little hole, and I saw the Germans coming and they killed the people where they jump. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was laying all day with the cabbage. Well, the boy didn't go with me. He went separately run away, run away. It was about 10 miles from the city. Because since I jump, the train was running around about 200 speed limit, I moved away the train about 10 miles. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started to go home to my father. I know my father was still alive because I want to tell them what happened to the mother and I want-- go in two weeks, I was going home to the city where I was born, lived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- two weeks, took me. There's one small town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was Germans there. They came with a big light reflector. You know what's mean, reflector? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because the Polish people, the bastards, they let the German know Jewish boy is going in the city, in the small town there. And they want me-- this reflector, they're looking for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or I running downstairs because was-- I run downstairs. They shouldn't-- they didn't see me, or their reflector was lighting but then 15 minutes, they didn't saw me. And I was going home. Still going home. I came home and saw my father. And there was about 200 people more, Jewish people, living in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I told they, they took the woman to kill, and the kids. They didn't believe me. They didn't believe me. I can't help it. I had no right to stay with them. Because they had papers to stay in the ghetto. And when the Germans will come in the daytime, they will see me. They will kill my father, and they will kill me. I had to go away in the woods. It was about 10 miles from the city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [? Valevkevelte, ?] then name. It was very thick, the woods. I was there. It was October. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had to make a place where to sleep. Oh. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But then 12 boys showed more. And they came to the woods which I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had been living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They jumped from the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were in Miedzyrecz, and they came to the woods after they was also walking two weeks. They came to the city where my father was, and they lived with me together. We digged a hole in the ground that maybe made that place for to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] All right. I can't help it. My father didn't want to believe it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had three brothers. One brother was with me, the youngest. He said I'm gonna go back to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Layzar. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he went to Father in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] Polish people came to me and said, you have to go to your father and tell them, they-- they come Wednesday. They're gonna kill him Wednesday. That was Friday, Saturday night. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And Wednesday, the Germans coming, they're gonna kill them one by one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the ghetto. I saw my father. I saw a lot more people, Jewish people, and I told them to run away, because Wednesday, they're gonna come and kill you. Oh yes, they're gonna come. They're going to come to you. And it started to snow. Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, it was snowing. Wednesday, the Germans came and killed them one by one. One brother run away. Dufche. He run away. But the Germans run after him and they killed him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right. No more family. Every-- everybody's gone. Killed everybody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was in the woods about a year and a half. It was [? Valevkevelte ?]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was another family which they had digged a hole, and they lived there. There was, for example, a difference from my place which I digged a hole, and they digged was about four miles away difference. The Germans came, surrendered them, and they killed them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I, myself, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I have a dream. You know what's in that dream? I dream my father came to me. He was dead already. He was killed already. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said, Sam, the Germans surrendered you. Just-- in a dream. Just like I talk to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was day, started daytime-- such a terrible dream. I lifted the door and looked out. And I saw the Germans. I saw the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They didn't saw me. It was dark already. The-- the hole which I opened up, I'd left too big. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I had a dream I had a gun. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had-- Yablonski, the name was Yablonski. I said, Mr. Yablonski, if you wouldn't give me a gun, I'm not-- I'm not surviving. You have to give me a gun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he gave me a gun. He gave me a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish gun with French bullets. And can't do nothing with French bullets. Oh, I said to myself, I'm gonna make Polish bullets. I used to know what the Germans used to learn how to shoot. You know how to shoot somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the bullets, the-- the-- what they call, the-- what they the bullets, the bullets-- they were-- they learned to-- how to catch. And the bullets they used to throw away. I was in the city. I said to myself, I was a very bright boy. I'm gonna go pick up the bullets what the German used to throw away the bullets. I went in the middle of the night, and I picked up [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 10 dozen bullets, and I brought them over to  the woods, where we used to live. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to make from the French, Polish bullets. If not, they wouldn't fit into-- in the gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want a Polish man. They gave me a file. A good file. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I filed around the pistol. Every bullet is a pistol-- they call it pistol, I think. See. I filed around the pistol. And took out-- easy, easy the pistol, and put it in the Polish bullets. I have bullets ready. I put it the [POLISH]-- you know what I call the [POLISH]? The powder. I put it in the Polish bullets. I made about a dozen bullets. Do you know, it was something unbelievable. The people that used to be with me, they used to watch me. You know? They used to watch me. Nobody said kill me, too, because they couldn't find another such a bright boy. Can we take a moment and talk a little bit about the living conditions in the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. In the wintertime, we-- I use to live in the-- in the-- in the hole. For example, I-- when I used to-- I used to have food to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop now and talk about it in the next tape.
